questions.prompt: A brand-new build just landed. Before doing anything else, you verify that login, add-to-cart, and checkout all load without crashing. Which check is this?
questions.explanation: Checking core flows broadly to decide if the build is worth testing at all is a smoke test: shallow and wide, run first.

questions.prompt: A developer adds a coupon field and says it's ready. You test only the coupon area in depth: valid, invalid, and expired codes. You don't test the rest of the app. What is this?
questions.explanation: Sanity testing is narrow-and-deep: a focused check that one specific changed area behaves correctly, without re-covering the whole build.

questions.prompt: Defect BS-014 was marked fixed. You re-run the exact reproduction steps from the original bug report to confirm. Which check is this?
questions.explanation: Re-running the exact failed case to confirm a specific fix is a retest. It targets one defect, not the wider system.

questions.prompt: The coupon change touched the totals calculation. You run the broader cart/checkout suite and discover free shipping no longer applies. What kind of testing surfaced this?
questions.explanation: Regression testing re-runs a broad suite to catch side effects a change caused elsewhere, like free shipping breaking after a totals change.

questions.prompt: BuggyShop v1.1 shipped, and bugs BS-021 and BS-022 were fixed. Why should permanent regression cases be added for them?
options: To reduce the number of smoke tests needed next release
So the same breaks can't silently return when future code changes
Because retests must always become regression cases by policy
To prove the original developers wrote bad code
questions.explanation: Each shipped-and-fixed bug becomes a permanent regression case so a later change cannot reintroduce it unnoticed. That is why suites grow over time.
